title: Physical Link Down – Optical Signal Lost
description: Interface operational status is down while administrative status is up. Loss of Signal (LOS) alarm present.

symptoms: LOS / Loss of Signal alarm on transceiver
log_patterns: %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface GigabitEthernet0/1, changed state to down

root_cause_analysis: Complete loss of optical signal between the local SFP and remote peer. Commonly caused by physical cable disconnect, fibre cut, faulty SFP/transceiver, or dirty connectors.

generic_steps: 1. Verify physical cable connection at both ends.
2. Inspect fibre patch cable for visible damage.
3. Clean optical connectors.
4. Check optical Tx/Rx power levels.
5. Swap SFP/transceiver with a known-good spare.
6. Engage physical infrastructure team for fibre testing if issue persists.

cisco.notes: On Catalyst 9000 check 'show platform software fed switch active ifm interfaces <interface>' for internal state.

artifacts.sample: %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface GigabitEthernet0/1, changed state to down

retrieval_text: Network Link Down. Interface oper_down admin_up, LOS alarm. Optical signal loss due to cable disconnect, fibre cut, or SFP failure. Check physical connectivity, transceiver power, swap SFP. Cisco/Fortinet/Huawei commands available.
reranker_text: Link down. Oper down, admin up, LOS. Optical signal loss: cable, SFP, fibre cut.

title: Interface Flapping – Intermittent Link State Changes
description: Interface repeatedly transitions between up and down causing network instability.

symptoms: Frequent link state up/down transitions in logs
log_patterns: %LINK-5-CHANGED: Interface GigabitEthernet0/2, changed state to down
%LINK-5-CHANGED: Interface GigabitEthernet0/2, changed state to up

root_cause_analysis: Marginal physical layer: dirty/damaged fibre, failing SFP that loses optical lock intermittently, loose connector, or auto-negotiation mismatch.

generic_steps: 1. Check system log for flapping interval and pattern.
2. Inspect and reseat fibre connectors and SFP.
3. Replace fibre patch cable.
4. Monitor optical Rx power for fluctuations.
5. Hard-code speed/duplex to rule out auto-negotiation.
6. Move connection to a different port to isolate hardware.

cisco.notes: Check errdisable recovery for link-flap with 'show errdisable detect'.

artifacts.sample:
%LINK-5-CHANGED: Interface Gi0/2, changed state to down
%LINK-5-CHANGED: Interface Gi0/2, changed state to up

retrieval_text: Network Link Flap. Intermittent up/down state changes. Rx power fluctuations, bad SFP/cable, auto-negotiation. Hard-code speed/duplex, replace cable or SFP. Vendor commands for Cisco/Fortinet/Huawei.
reranker_text: Link flap: repeated up/down, Rx power fluctuates, bad SFP or cable. Fix: hard-code speed/duplex, replace components.

title: High Interface Error Rate – Degraded Link Quality
description: Interface remains up/up but shows a rapidly increasing number of input/output errors or packet drops.

symptoms: Interface up/up, but high error counters (CRC, frame, runts, giants)

root_cause_analysis: Physical layer issues: faulty cable, failing transceiver, dirty connectors, EMI, or duplex mismatch. Can also be caused by a DDoS or excessive microbursts.

generic_steps: 1. Check interface error counters (CRC, frame, runts, giants).
2. Verify duplex/speed matches on both ends.
3. Replace patch cable and connectors.
4. Monitor optical power levels for fluctuations.
5. Inspect for electromagnetic interference (EMI).
6. Enable error correction features if available.

diagnostic_commands: get system interface physical <interface> | grep error

retrieval_text: Network Link High Error Rate. Interface up but rising CRC/input errors. Likely physical fault: cable, SFP, duplex mismatch, EMI. Replace cable, check duplex, clean optics.
reranker_text: High error rate: CRC/input errors. Caused by cable, SFP, duplex mismatch, EMI.

title: CRC Errors on Interface – Frame Corruption
description: Interface showing CRC errors only, while other errors remain low. Indicates frame corruption on the wire.
root_cause_analysis: CRC errors are typically caused by physical layer problems: bad cable, faulty transceiver, dirty fibre connectors, or electrical interference. It can also be a mismatched FEC setting on optical interfaces.

generic_steps: 1. Check CRC counter on the interface.
2. Clean optical connectors and inspect patch cable.
3. Swap SFP/transceiver.
4. Check if FEC (Forward Error Correction) is enabled and matches peer.
5. Test with a known-good cable directly between devices.

diagnostic_commands: get system interface physical <interface> | grep crc
diagnose hardware deviceinfo nic <interface> | grep rx_crc_errors

retrieval_text: Network Link CRC Errors. Only CRC errors seen, frame corruption. Caused by bad cable, optics, or FEC mismatch. Clean connectors, swap optics, verify FEC.
reranker_text: CRC errors: frame corruption, bad cable/optics, FEC mismatch.

title: Interface Flapping – Beyond Link Flap, Possibly Higher Layer
description: Interface state (line protocol) flapping even though physical link may stay up; often caused by keepalive or control plane issues.

symptoms: Possible keepalive failure, IP address conflict
log_patterns: %LINK-5-CHANGED: Interface Eth0/0, line protocol changed state to down
%LINEPROTO-5-UPDOWN: Line protocol on Interface Eth0/0, changed state to up

root_cause_analysis: Line protocol flapping with stable physical link suggests a Layer 2 issue: misconfigured VLAN, spanning-tree topology changes, duplicate IP, or police/rate limit drops causing keepalive failure.

generic_steps: 1. Check interface statistics for any dropped packets.
2. Verify VLAN membership and spanning-tree state.
3. Look for IP address conflicts on the subnet.
4. Disable keepalives temporarily to test.
5. Review logs for STP topology change notifications.

diagnostic_commands: get system interface physical <interface> | grep state

retrieval_text: Network Interface Flapping. Line protocol up/down but physical link up. Possible VLAN mismatch, STP, IP conflict, keepalive failure. Check VLAN, STP, IP conflicts.
reranker_text: Interface flapping: line protocol down, physical up. VLAN, STP, IP conflict, keepalive.

title: Interface Input Errors – Drops at Ingress
description: Interface is up but showing input errors (overruns, ignored packets) indicating receive buffer overflows.
root_cause_analysis: Typically caused by insufficient input buffers (hold-queue), bursty traffic exceeding interface capacity, or a microburst that overruns the hardware queue.

generic_steps: 1. Verify interface bandwidth utilisation and input drops.
2. Increase input hold-queue size (if supported).
3. Enable flow control if the device supports it.
4. Tune CoS/QoS to prioritise critical traffic.
5. Consider upgrading the link speed.

diagnostic_commands: get system interface physical <interface> | grep rx_errors
diagnose hardware deviceinfo nic <interface> | grep rx_missed_errors

retrieval_text: Network Interface Input Errors. Rising input errors due to buffer overflows. Increase hold-queue, enable flow control, or upgrade link.
reranker_text: Input errors: overruns, buffer overflow. Increase queue, flow control, upgrade.

title: BGP Peer Session Down
description: BGP state transitions from Established to Idle/Active/Connect. Neighbor not reachable or config mismatch.
root_cause_analysis: BGP down due to Layer 3 unreachability, ACL/firewall blocking TCP 179, BGP AS mismatch, hold timer mismatch, or MD5 authentication failure.

generic_steps: 1. Ping neighbor IP from source interface.
2. Check if TCP 179 is open (telnet / nc).
3. Verify BGP AS numbers match on both sides.
4. Ensure neighbor not shut administratively.
5. Check MD5 key if configured.

retrieval_text: Network Routing BGP Down. Neighbor not Established. Check L3 connectivity, TCP 179, AS numbers, authentication. Vendor-specific commands provided.
reranker_text: BGP down: TCP 179 blocked, AS mismatch. Fix connectivity and configuration.

title: OSPF Neighbor Down – Adjacency Lost
description: OSPF neighbor state leaves FULL. Possible interface or hello parameter mismatch.

log_patterns: %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.1 on Eth0 from FULL to DOWN

root_cause_analysis: Adjacency lost due to interface down, mismatched OSPF timers, mismatched MTU, network type mismatch (broadcast vs p2p), or authentication failure.

generic_steps: 1. Check underlying interface status.
2. Verify OSPF hello/dead timers match.
3. Compare MTU settings (ip ospf mtu-ignore).
4. Confirm network type (point-to-point/broadcast).
5. Check OSPF authentication keys.

artifacts.sample: %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.1 on Eth0 from FULL to DOWN

retrieval_text: Network Routing OSPF Down. Neighbor not FULL. Check interface, timers, MTU, authentication. Vendor commands for Cisco/Fortinet/Huawei.
reranker_text: OSPF neighbor down: timer mismatch, MTU, authentication.

title: Route Flapping – Unstable Routing Table
description: A specific route keeps appearing and disappearing from the routing table.
root_cause_analysis: Flapping route caused by unstable underlying link, frequent BGP/OSPF reconvergences, duplicate router ID, or route dampening settings too low.

generic_steps: 1. Identify the flapping route.
2. Check if the next hop is reachable and stable.
3. Look for routing loops or duplicate router IDs.
4. Adjust route dampening parameters if using BGP.
5. Stabilise the underlying IGP.

retrieval_text: Network Route Flapping. Route appears/withdraws frequently. Check next-hop stability, duplicate router IDs, enable BGP dampening.
reranker_text: Route flapping: next-hop unstable, router ID duplicate, enable dampening.

title: Network Device Unreachable – Switch/Router Down
description: Device stops responding to SNMP/ping and all monitored interfaces show down.
root_cause_analysis: Power failure, hardware crash, IOS/FortiOS crash (kernel panic), total fibre cut to management interface, or device in boot loop.

generic_steps: 1. Check physical power (PSU, power strip).
2. Verify console access via out-of-band.
3. Inspect hardware alarms if reachable out-of-band.
4. Remote power cycle (if PDU available).
5. Check upstream device logs for link down on connected ports.

retrieval_text: Network Device Down. No response, all interfaces down. Check power, console, upstream links.
reranker_text: Device down: power failure, crash, boot loop.

title: High CPU Usage on Network Device
description: Device CPU pegged at 100%, causing slow CLI, routing protocol flaps.
root_cause_analysis: High CPU can be due to process (e.g., BGP scanner, SNMP poller), control plane DDoS, or a loop/spanning-tree issue causing constant topology changes.

generic_steps: 1. Identify top CPU-using process.
2. Check for spanning-tree topology changes.
3. Rate-limit management plane traffic (CoPP).
4. Optimise SNMP polling intervals.
5. Disable unused services.

diagnostic_commands: show spanning-tree detail | include topology change

retrieval_text: Network Device High CPU. Identify process, STP changes, apply CoPP. Commands for Cisco/Fortinet/Huawei.
reranker_text: High CPU: process hog, STP loop, DDoS. Apply rate-limiting.

title: Traffic Dropped by Firewall Policy
description: Legitimate traffic is being dropped by an implicit deny or misconfigured rule.

symptoms: Packet capture shows SYN sent, no SYN-ACK
Firewall logs show deny action for expected traffic
log_patterns: Deny: src=10.10.10.1 dst=10.20.20.1 proto=TCP sport=443 dport=8443

root_cause_analysis: Missing firewall rule, incorrect policy ordering, or the traffic is matching an explicit deny before the allow rule.

generic_steps: 1. Identify the dropped flow from firewall logs.
2. Verify the rule base order – explicit deny rules may come first.
3. Add a specific allow rule above the deny.
4. Test connectivity after change.

remediation_commands: access-list OUTSIDE_IN extended permit tcp host <src> host <dst> eq <dport>

diagnostic_commands: diag sniffer packet any 'host <src> and host <dst>' 4
diag debug flow show console enable

retrieval_text: Network Firewall Policy Drop. Traffic dropped by firewall rule. Check access lists, policy order. Add allow rule. Commands for ASA/FTD/FortiGate/Huawei USG.
reranker_text: Firewall drop: missing rule, policy order. Add allow rule.

title: IPsec VPN Tunnel Down – Phase 1 or Phase 2 Failure
description: Site‑to‑site VPN tunnel is not established or drops frequently.
root_cause_analysis: Common causes: IKE/IPsec proposal mismatch, pre‑shared key wrong, certificate expired, peer unreachable, or NAT traversal not configured.

generic_steps: 1. Verify peer is reachable from the public IP.
2. Compare IKE and IPsec policies on both ends.
3. Confirm pre‑shared keys match.
4. Enable NAT‑T and DPD.
5. Check certificate validity if using PKI.

retrieval_text: Network VPN Tunnel Down. Phase1/Phase2 negotiation failed. Check proposals, PSK, NAT-T. Cisco/FortiGate/Huawei USG commands included.
reranker_text: VPN down: proposal mismatch, PSK wrong, peer unreachable.

title: High CPU Usage on Linux/Windows Server
description: CPU utilisation consistently above 90%, causing application slowness.
root_cause_analysis: Runaway process, insufficient resources for workload, CPU throttling in virtualized environment, or malware.

generic_steps: 1. Top/htop to identify high CPU process.
2. Check for CPU steal time in VM.
3. Increase CPU resources or scale out.
4. Kill/restart runaway process if applicable.

retrieval_text: Compute CPU High Usage. Runaway process, CPU steal, or undersized VM. Use top/ps to identify, kill or scale up.
reranker_text: High CPU: process, CPU steal, scale.

title: CPU Soft Lockup – Kernel Hung Task
description: Linux kernel reports soft lockup, server becomes unresponsive.

symptoms: Kernel messages: 'BUG: soft lockup - CPU#X stuck'

root_cause_analysis: Kernel bug, driver issue (e.g., NVMe), or CPU overcommit/hypervisor problem causing vCPU starvation.

generic_steps: 1. Check console (IPMI/iDRAC) for kernel dump.
2. Review hardware health (memory, CPU).
3. Update kernel or drivers.
4. Hard reboot if necessary.

retrieval_text: Compute CPU Soft Lockup. Kernel bug, vCPU starvation. Check console, update kernel, reboot.
reranker_text: Soft lockup: kernel bug, vCPU issue. Reboot, update.

title: Memory Exhaustion – OOM Killer Triggered
description: System runs out of RAM, OOM killer terminates processes.

symptoms: Free memory near zero, high swap usage

root_cause_analysis: Memory leak in application, insufficient RAM for workload, or system cache not released.

generic_steps: 1. Identify process with highest memory (top/htop).
2. Check for memory leak (growing RSS).
3. Restart the leaking service.
4. Add more RAM or scale application instances.

retrieval_text: Compute Memory Exhaustion. OOM killer active. Memory leak or high load. Restart service, increase RAM.
reranker_text: Memory exhaustion: OOM, leak. Restart, add memory.

title: Gradual Memory Leak in Application
description: Process memory usage grows over time without releasing.

symptoms: Steady increase in RSS of a process

root_cause_analysis: Application bug causing memory not freed. Common in Java apps with heap leaks, or native code leaks.

generic_steps: 1. Use pmap/memory profiler to identify leak source.
2. Restart the application periodically as workaround.
3. Apply application patch.

retrieval_text: Compute Memory Leak. Application RSS grows over time. Profile, restart periodically, patch.
reranker_text: Memory leak: RSS grows, restart, patch.

title: Disk Partition Full – No Space Left on Device
description: File system utilisation reaches 100%, causing write errors.
root_cause_analysis: Log rotation not working, large core dump, unattended file growth, or insufficient disk partitioning.

generic_steps: 1. Identify largest files/directories with du.
2. Clean old logs, core dumps, temp files.
3. Rotate/compress logs.
4. Resize filesystem if possible.

diagnostic_commands: Get-ChildItem C:\ -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Sort-Object Length -Descending | Select -First 10

retrieval_text: Compute Disk Full. 100% utilisation. Clean logs, core dumps, resize partition. Commands for Linux and Windows.
reranker_text: Disk full: clean logs, resize.

title: Disk I/O Errors – Bad Sectors or Controller Fault
description: Kernel reports I/O errors on a disk device, causing file system errors.
root_cause_analysis: Failing hard disk (bad sectors), loose cable, faulty RAID controller, or NVMe media errors.

generic_steps: 1. Check SMART attributes for the drive.
2. Inspect physical connections.
3. Replace failing disk if under warranty.
4. Restore from backup if filesystem is corrupted.

retrieval_text: Compute Disk I/O Errors. Failing disk or controller. Check SMART, replace disk.
reranker_text: Disk IO error: bad sectors, hardware failure.

title: Critical Process Crashed – Core Dump
description: A monitored process terminated unexpectedly, leaving a core dump.
root_cause_analysis: Segmentation fault, out-of-memory (but process killed), unhandled exception, or bug.

generic_steps: 1. Examine core dump with gdb to find crash location.
2. Check system logs for OOM/segfault messages.
3. Restart the process and monitor.
4. Apply application patch or increase memory limits.

retrieval_text: Compute Process Crash. Segfault or unhandled exception. Analyse core dump, restart service.
reranker_text: Process crash: segfault, core dump. Restart, analyse.

title: Zombie Processes Accumulation
description: Defunct child processes not reaped, consuming process table slots.

symptoms: System may have high load but low CPU
Unable to fork new processes when PID limit reached

root_cause_analysis: Parent process not calling wait() on terminated children. Often a programming error or parent stuck in sleep.

generic_steps: 1. Identify parent of zombie processes.
2. Restart the misbehaving parent process.
3. Apply fix to application to properly reap children.

retrieval_text: Compute Process Zombie. Defunct children. Restart parent process. Check application code.
reranker_text: Zombie processes: parent not reaping. Restart parent.

title: Storage Volume Goes Offline – LUN Not Accessible
description: A storage volume/LUN is no longer visible to the host, causing I/O errors.

symptoms: Volume not listed in /dev/mapper or disk management

root_cause_analysis: SAN fabric zoning issue, storage controller failover not working, HBA/cable failure, or LUN unpresentation.

generic_steps: 1. Check HBA link status.
2. Verify zoning on SAN switches (Cisco MDS, Brocade).
3. Rescan SCSI bus.
4. Check storage array for LUN mapping.

retrieval_text: Storage Volume Offline. LUN not accessible, path failures. Rescan SCSI, check zoning, HBA.
reranker_text: Volume offline: HBA, zoning, multipath failure.

title: Storage Volume High Latency – Very Slow I/O
description: Read/write operations take seconds instead of milliseconds. Application timeouts.
root_cause_analysis: Underlying storage array overloaded, network congestion (iSCSI/NFS), faulty drive in RAID group causing rebuild, or misaligned partitions.

generic_steps: 1. Check iostat to identify the busy device.
2. On the storage array, check for disk failure/rebuild.
3. Verify network latency for iSCSI or FC errors.
4. Tune queue depth and I/O scheduler.

retrieval_text: Storage Volume Slow Performance. High await, queue depth, possible array issue. Tune scheduler, check array for failure.
reranker_text: Slow storage: high latency, array rebuild, network issues.

title: RAID Array Degraded – One or More Member Disks Failed
description: RAID controller reports array in degraded state, hot spare activated.
root_cause_analysis: Physical disk failure. SMART predictive failure often warns beforehand. Loose disk or backplane issue can also cause transient fault.

generic_steps: 1. Identify the failed disk (e.g., /dev/sdb).
2. Confirm it is physically dead (LED, SMART).
3. Replace the disk and start rebuild.
4. Monitor rebuild progress.

retrieval_text: Storage RAID Degraded. Physical disk failure. Replace failed drive, start rebuild. mdadm or hardware RAID manager.
reranker_text: RAID degraded: disk failure. Replace, rebuild.

title: RAID Rebuild Failed or Stuck
description: After replacing a disk, rebuild either stops or fails, leaving array degraded.

log_patterns: md/raid:md0: Disk failure on sdb, disabling device.

root_cause_analysis: New disk is also faulty, backplane issue causing write errors, or incompatible firmware. Insufficient power causing second disk failure.

generic_steps: 1. Check dmesg for new I/O errors on the replacement disk.
2. Verify the new disk is same size or larger and not faulty (SMART).
3. Try a different slot or cable.
4. If rebuild aborted, zero superblock and re-add.

retrieval_text: Storage RAID Rebuild Failure. Stuck rebuild due to bad replacement disk. Check SMART, zero superblock, re-add.
reranker_text: RAID rebuild fail: bad disk, backplane. Zero superblock, re-add.

title: SAN Fabric Zoning Misconfiguration – Host Cannot See LUN
description: Host lost connectivity to storage LUN after a zoning change or switch reboot.
root_cause_analysis: Incorrect zone configuration, member port not added to zone, or zone set not activated. Caused by human error during maintenance.

generic_steps: 1. Verify WWN of host HBA and storage port.
2. Check active zoneset on the fabric switch.
3. Add missing member and commit zoneset.
4. Rescan on host.

retrieval_text: SAN Fabric Zoning Issue. LUN not visible after zoning change. Check active zoneset, add WWNs.
reranker_text: Zoning issue: WWN missing from zone. Update zoneset.

title: Application Service Not Running – Daemon Stopped
description: Monitored service (e.g., nginx, apache) is not responding on its port.
root_cause_analysis: Service crashed, out-of-memory, configuration error preventing start, or manual stop.

generic_steps: 1. Check service status with systemctl/init.d.
2. Look at service logs (journalctl) for crash reason.
3. Restart the service.
4. Investigate root cause (memory limit, config syntax).

retrieval_text: Application Service Down. Service not listening. Check status, logs, restart. Usually due to crash or config error.
reranker_text: Service down: crash, config. Restart.

title: Service Responding Slowly – High Latency
description: Service is up but response time is significantly higher than baseline.
root_cause_analysis: Database bottleneck, upstream service degradation, GC pauses (Java), or insufficient worker threads.

generic_steps: 1. Identify slow internal component using APM/tracing.
2. Check database connection pool and query times.
3. Increase thread/worker count if pool exhausted.
4. Scale up/out application instances.

retrieval_text: Application Service Slow. High response time. Check dependencies (DB, cache). Increase threads/scale.
reranker_text: Service slow: DB, threads, scale.

title: Database Connection Pool Exhausted
description: Application cannot obtain a DB connection from the pool, requests fail.

symptoms: Logs: 'Cannot get JDBC connection' or 'pool exhausted'

root_cause_analysis: Connection leak (not returning to pool), high concurrent requests, or long-running queries blocking connections.

generic_steps: 1. Identify top queries causing long holds.
2. Kill stuck connections or restart pool.
3. Increase pool maximum size.
4. Fix connection leak in application code.

remediation_commands: SELECT pg_terminate_backend(pid) FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state = 'idle' AND state_change < now() - interval '10 minutes';

retrieval_text: Database Connection Pool Exhausted. No free connections. Long queries, leaks. Terminate idle connections, increase pool size.
reranker_text: Conn pool exhausted: leak, long queries. Kill idle, increase pool.

title: Database Deadlock – Transactions Blocking Each Other
description: Two or more transactions are stuck waiting for locks held by each other.

log_patterns: Deadlock found when trying to get lock

root_cause_analysis: Conflicting lock ordering between concurrent transactions. Often caused by two updates touching the same rows in reverse order.

generic_steps: 1. Identify the transactions involved using deadlock graph (SQL Server extended events, PostgreSQL log).
2. Tune application queries to always lock in same order.
3. Reduce transaction length.
4. Use lower isolation level (READ COMMITTED).

retrieval_text: Database Deadlock. Conflicting locks. Analyse deadlock graph, fix lock order, reduce transaction length.
reranker_text: Deadlock: lock order conflict. Fix query order.

title: 5xx HTTP Error Rate Spike
description: Web server returns 500/502/503 errors for a large percentage of requests.
root_cause_analysis: Backend application down or overloaded (502), or web server out of workers (503). Could also be a misconfigured proxy timeout.

generic_steps: 1. Check backend service health (is it responding?).
2. Tune proxy timeouts (nginx proxy_read_timeout).
3. Restart or scale up backend instances.
4. Check server resources (CPU/memory).

retrieval_text: Application Web 5xx Error Spike. High rate of 5xx errors. Backend issues or proxy misconfiguration. Check backend health, adjust timeouts.
reranker_text: 5xx spike: backend down, timeout. Check backend, adjust proxy.

title: TLS/SSL Handshake Failure – Certificate Issues
description: Clients receiving SSL handshake errors, site unreachable via HTTPS.

symptoms: OpenSSL s_client returns alert certificate expired
Web server log: SSL handshake failed

root_cause_analysis: Certificate expired, cipher suite mismatch (client doesn't support server's ciphers), or missing intermediate CA.

generic_steps: 1. Check certificate expiry date.
2. Validate certificate chain using online tool.
3. Update cipher suites to include modern ones.
4. Reinstall certificate and restart web server.

retrieval_text: Application Web TLS Handshake Failure. Certificate expiry, cipher mismatch. Check expiry, update ciphers.
reranker_text: TLS fail: cert expired, cipher mismatch.

title: DDoS Attack – High Inbound Traffic Spikes
description: Sudden surge in traffic from multiple sources, saturating network bandwidth or connection tables.

symptoms: Interface input rate jumps to near line rate

root_cause_analysis: Volumetric DDoS (UDP/ICMP flood), SYN flood, or application‑layer attack (HTTP flood). Often originates from botnets.

generic_steps: 1. Enable rate limiting / traffic scrubbing if available.
2. Blackhole route the target IP via upstream ISP (RTBH).
3. Increase connection table limits on firewall.
4. Engage DDoS mitigation service (Cloudflare, AWS Shield).

retrieval_text: Security DDoS Attack. Traffic surge. Enable DoS policies, blackhole routing, engage scrubbing service.
reranker_text: DDoS: bandwidth saturation, SYN flood. Blackhole, rate limit.

title: Brute Force Login Attack – Repeated Authentication Failures
description: Multiple failed login attempts for SSH/RDP/web applications from a single or distributed sources.

symptoms: Auth logs show many failed attempts for same user or admin user
Sudden increase in auth failure metrics
log_patterns: Failed password for admin from 192.168.1.100 port 22

root_cause_analysis: An attacker is trying to guess credentials, often from a botnet or script.

generic_steps: 1. Block attacker IP temporarily with iptables/fail2ban.
2. Disable root login over SSH, use key‑only auth.
3. Implement rate limiting (OpenSSH MaxStartups, fail2ban).
4. Monitor for further attempts.

diagnostic_commands: grep 'Failed password' /var/log/auth.log | awk '{print $(NF-3)}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr

retrieval_text: Security Brute Force Login. Many failed auth attempts. Block IPs with fail2ban/iptables. Disable password login.
reranker_text: Brute force: auth failures. Block IPs, rate limit.

title: Security Policy Violation – Unauthorised Access Attempt
description: An internal host attempted to connect to a blocked category or port, triggering a security alert.

symptoms: Firewall alert: traffic denied by security policy
Infection or user trying to bypass policy

root_cause_analysis: User attempting to access prohibited resources (e.g., gambling, malware site) or a compromised internal host phoning home.

generic_steps: 1. Investigate the source IP and user.
2. If user policy violation, warn or restrict further.
3. If malware indication, isolate the host and run antivirus scan.

retrieval_text: Security Policy Violation. Unauthorised access attempt. Investigate user/host, enforce policy, isolate if malware.
reranker_text: Policy violation: blocked site, malware. Investigate, isolate.
